Letter

Constrained isomeric richness reveals a latitudinal gradient in hidden DOM structural complexity

[Author A]1,*, [Author B]1, [Author C]2
1[Institution]   2[Institution]
*Correspondence: [email]

Abstract

Each molecular formula detected by Fourier-transform ion cyclotron resonance mass spectrometry (FT-ICR-MS) in dissolved organic matter (DOM) may represent multiple structural isomers with distinct ecological functions—a hidden reservoir we term the ‘DOM clonome’. Trapped ion mobility spectrometry has confirmed 6–40 isomers per formula, but does not scale to large surveys. We introduce Constrained Isomeric Richness (CIR), a per-formula index that estimates the number of chemically plausible isomers using compound-class–specific structural constraints anchored to published mobility measurements. Applied to 8,972 formulae from 27 river sites in the WHONDRS network and analysed at the site level (n = 27), CIR reveals three findings. First, isomeric depth varies systematically by compound class: lipid-like formulae harbour ~19 estimated isomers and aromatic classes ~8, consistent with known structural chemistry. Second, mean CIR of the CRAM fraction shows the strongest correlation with latitude among all tested metrics (r = −0.79, p < 10−5, n = 27), slightly exceeding mean H/C (r = −0.73). Third, CIR is negatively correlated with formula richness (r = −0.40, p = 0.039, n = 27), showing that structural depth and compositional breadth are partially decoupled dimensions of DOM complexity. CIR is analytically reproducible (replicate CV = 0.32%) and computationally inexpensive. Applied without modification to six additional datasets spanning river, sediment, soil, Arctic, and marine environments (691,229 total formulae across 1,343 samples), CIR reproduced the same compound class ordering in every case. CRAM isomeric depth followed a gradient consistent with processing intensity: Arctic < sediment < river < soil < marine.

Introduction

The DOM clonome and the isomeric blind spot

Dissolved organic matter (DOM) fuels aquatic biogeochemistry, yet its true structural complexity remains largely invisible. Ultrahigh-resolution mass spectrometry (FT-ICR-MS) has transformed our understanding of DOM by resolving it into thousands of distinct molecular formulae (Stenson et al. 2003; Kujawinski 2002). However, this view is fundamentally incomplete because FT-ICR-MS detects exact masses, not structural architectures. Each formula may correspond to multiple constitutional isomers—molecules sharing atomic composition but differing in bond connectivity, ring configuration, and functional group placement. These structural differences matter: isomers of a given formula can differ in biodegradability (Ohno et al. 2014), photoreactivity (Stubbins et al. 2010), and metal-binding capacity (Aiken et al. 2011).

Trapped ion mobility spectrometry coupled to FT-ICR-MS (TIMS-FT-ICR-MS) has made this hidden diversity directly observable. Leyva et al. (2019, 2020) showed that individual DOM formulae resolve into 6–40 mobility-separated peaks, each representing a structurally different isomer. By analogy to immunology—where a single phenotypic marker hides a diverse repertoire of T cell clones (Qi et al. 2014)—we conceptualise this hidden structural reservoir as the ‘DOM clonome’. However, TIMS requires specialised instrumentation available in very few laboratories, making it impractical for large-scale environmental surveys.

Estimating Constrained Isomeric Richness

Full constitutional isomer enumeration is computationally intractable for DOM-sized molecules (Benecke et al. 1997), but most theoretical isomers are chemically implausible in natural waters. DOM derives from a limited set of biosynthetic precursors—lignin, terpenoids, proteins, carbohydrates (Dittmar and Stubbins 2014)—and aquatic chemistry imposes additional constraints. We introduce Constrained Isomeric Richness (CIR), a heuristic index that estimates the size of the chemically plausible isomer subset by scoring structural degrees of freedom after applying compound-class–specific constraints. The penalty structure reflects fundamental chemical expectations: carbohydrate-like formulae are expected to have fewer plausible constitutional arrangements than lipid-like formulae, because extensive oxygenation and required ring formations constrain the space of viable structures. CIR translates these expectations into a quantifiable, per-formula metric of structural depth, anchored to the TIMS-observed range.

Study objectives

We applied CIR to 8,972 formulae across 80 replicate samples (27 sites) from the WHONDRS 2018 surface water campaign (Stegen et al. 2022; Goldman et al. 2020), with all environmental correlations computed at the site level (n = 27) to avoid pseudoreplication. We then tested cross-ecosystem portability by applying the same pipeline, without parameter adjustment, to WHONDRS Summer 2019 sediment porewater (127,136 formulae from 276 samples, assigned from raw XML peak lists using a Python formula assignment engine) and marine DOM from the Weddell Sea and North Sea (30,198 formulae; Moye et al. 2025). We tested whether: (1) CIR varies among compound classes consistently with structural chemistry across ecosystem types; (2) CIR correlates with environmental gradients more strongly than simpler metrics; and (3) CIR is partially independent of conventional formula richness.

Methods

Study system

Surface water triplicate samples from 27 river sites across eight U.S. states (30.2°–46.4°N; WHONDRS 2018 campaign; Stegen et al. 2022; Goldman et al. 2020). Water temperature: 6–63°C (the 63°C site, S000022, is likely geothermally influenced and was retained in analyses; excluding it did not qualitatively alter results). DOC: 0.9–5.2 mg L−1.

FT-ICR-MS

12-Tesla FT-ICR-MS at EMSL (Tfaily et al. 2015). Formula constraints: C1–57H1–100O0–30N0–5S0–3P0–2; mass error <1 ppm (Koch et al. 2007). Of 34,021 features, 8,972 received assignments.

Compound class assignment

Indices: DBE = 1 + C − H/2 + N/2; AImod (Koch and Dittmar 2006); NOSC (LaRowe and Van Cappellen 2011). Classes were assigned in the following priority order (first match wins): (1) condensed aromatic (AImod ≥ 0.66); (2) aromatic (0.50 ≤ AImod < 0.66); (3) CRAM (H/C < 1.5, O/C < 0.5; Hertkorn et al. 2006); (4) carbohydrate-like (H/C ≥ 1.5, O/C ≥ 0.5); (5) lipid-like (H/C ≥ 1.5, O/C < 0.5); (6) protein-like (N > 0, H/C > 1.3); (7) N-containing (N > 0); (8) unclassified. The priority order resolves overlaps; e.g., a formula with N > 0 and AImod ≥ 0.66 is assigned to condensed aromatic, not N-containing.

CIR scoring function

The complete CIR equation, with all coefficients:

log10(CIR)raw = 0.8·log10(max(1, C−3))  +  max(0.1, −0.15(DBE−4)2 + 1.2)
        +  wO·log10(max(1, O))  +  0.5·log10(max(1, N+1))
        −  0.12·nreq  −  0.05·nforb  −  Parom  −  Pring

where wO = 0.4 for carbohydrate-like, 0.7 otherwise; nreq and nforb are the number of required and forbidden substructures for the assigned class; Parom = AImod·0.8 for aromatic classes (0 otherwise); and Pring = max(0, DBE − Rmax)·0.1 where Rmax is the class-specific maximum ring count.

Chemically disfavoured features (all classes): peroxide bonds, triple bonds, 3-membered rings, 4-membered rings. These were not explicitly enumerated as candidate structures; instead, their implausibility was encoded as score penalties within the heuristic (each disfavoured feature reduces the score by 0.05). Required substructures (class-specific; e.g., aromatic ring for aromatic classes, carboxyl for CRAM with O ≥ 2) were similarly encoded as penalties (−0.12 each), reflecting the reduction in structural freedom when a specific functional group must be present.

The raw score is linearly rescaled to anchor against published TIMS observations:

log10(CIR) = 0.78 + (log10(CIR)raw / 3.0) × 0.82,   clipped to [0.3, 1.70]

The constants 0.78 and 1.60 correspond to log10(6) and log10(40), the approximate bounds of isomer counts observed by TIMS-FT-ICR-MS (Leyva et al. 2019). The lower clip (0.3) provides a floor for extreme cases; the upper clip (1.70) provides a modest safety margin above the TIMS ceiling. In practice, no formula in our dataset exceeded 1.58 and only three reached the lower bound. This anchoring ensures CIR values fall in or near the empirically observed range but does not constitute independent validation of accuracy; it is a calibration choice.

Aggregation and statistics

Formula-level CIR was aggregated as intensity-weighted mean log10(CIR) per sample (Tfaily et al. 2015), then averaged across triplicates to yield site-level means. All environmental correlations are reported at the site level (n = 27) to avoid pseudoreplication, since latitude, temperature, and DOC are site-level variables. Replicate-level statistics (n = 80) are reported only for replicate precision. Pearson and Spearman coefficients were used. Leave-one-site-out cross-validation assessed robustness. The pipeline (Python 3.12, 306 lines, open-source) runs in ~2 s.

Results

Compound classes and formula-level CIR

Table 1. CIR by compound class (log10 scale), ordered by median. All 8,972 assigned formulae included.

Compound classn%MeanSDMedian~Isomers
Aromatic1,13612.70.8760.0550.8817.6
Condensed aromatic4465.00.8830.0880.8967.9
CRAM2,73530.50.9980.0900.9889.7
Protein-like2052.31.1240.0901.08612.2
Carbohydrate-like95110.61.1730.1131.19915.8
N-containing4725.31.2310.0421.23717.3
Unclassified1,80220.11.2570.0611.25217.8
Lipid-like1,22513.71.2600.1261.26718.5
Total8,972100.0

CIR varied systematically across compound classes (Fig. 1A; Table 1). Aromatic classes (aromatic and condensed aromatic) exhibited the lowest median CIR (~8 estimated isomers per formula), consistent with the geometric constraints of ring fusion (Koch and Dittmar 2006). Lipid-like formulae scored highest (~19 isomers), reflecting the many ways a long aliphatic chain can be branched, hydroxylated, and esterified. CRAM was intermediate (~10 isomers). The CIR range (0.30–1.58) was anchored to the TIMS-observed range of 6–40 isomers (Leyva et al. 2019); no assigned formulae produced undefined values.

Site-level environmental correlations

Table 2. Correlations between CIR and environmental variables at the site level (n = 27 site means).

VariableCIR metricrp
LatitudeMean CIR−0.3970.040
TemperatureMean CIR0.2710.17
DOCMean CIR0.1110.58
Formula richnessMean CIR−0.4000.039
LatitudeCIRCRAM−0.785<10−5
TemperatureCIRCRAM0.631<0.001
DOCCIRCRAM0.4260.027

CIRCRAM showed the strongest environmental signal (Table 2). The site-level latitude correlation (r = −0.79, p < 10−5) was the strongest among the tested summary metrics, including mean H/C of the CRAM fraction (r = −0.73), mean DBE (r = 0.71), and mean molecular weight (r = 0.67; Table 3). The margin over mean H/C is modest (|Δr| = 0.06), suggesting CIR integrates multiple chemical dimensions into a slightly more informative composite, but is not dramatically superior to well-chosen single metrics. Leave-one-site-out cross-validation yielded r values between −0.73 and −0.83, with no single site driving the result.

Bulk mean CIR correlated with latitude (r = −0.40, p = 0.040) but not significantly with temperature (r = 0.27, p = 0.17) or DOC (r = 0.11, p = 0.58) at the site level.

Table 3. Comparison of CIRCRAM with simpler CRAM-fraction metrics for latitude prediction (n = 27).

Metric|r| vs. latitudep
CIRCRAM0.785<10−5
Mean H/C (CRAM)0.726<10−4
Mean DBE (CRAM)0.711<10−4
Mean mass (CRAM)0.667<10−3
Mean AImod (CRAM)0.643<10−3

Independence from formula richness

Site-level mean CIR and formula richness were negatively correlated (r = −0.40, p = 0.039, n = 27). To test whether this was driven by noisy low-intensity peaks, we restricted the analysis to formulae present in all three replicates per site (reducing the mean formulae per site from 2,245 to 1,592), recomputed site-level means, and retested the site-level correlation. The result was unchanged (r = −0.42, p = 0.030, n = 27). The partial decoupling of structural depth and compositional breadth is therefore a genuine ecological pattern, not an artefact of detection sensitivity.

Replicate precision

Mean CV of CIR across triplicates: 0.32% (27 sites).

Fig. 1. CIR across 27 WHONDRS river sites. (A) CIR by compound class (formula level). (B) Van Krevelen diagram coloured by CIR. (C) Site-level CIR distribution (n = 27 site means). (D–F) Site mean CIR vs. temperature, latitude, and DOC; each point is one site (triplicate mean); regression lines fitted to site means.

Cross-ecosystem application

To test portability, we applied the same pipeline without parameter adjustment to six additional FT-ICR-MS datasets spanning river, sediment, soil, Arctic, and marine environments (Table 4): WHONDRS S19S 2019 surface water (166,262 formulae from 282 samples at 97 globally distributed river sites); WHONDRS S19S 2019 sediment porewater (127,136 formulae, 276 samples); WHONDRS WROL 2019 watershed resilience campaign (150,999 formulae, 501 samples); Yukon Delta Experiment 2021 Arctic sediment (86,338 formulae, 180 samples); soil DOM from a Chinese paddy field fertilisation experiment (121,324 formulae, 21 samples; Xia and Liu 2025); and marine DOM from the Weddell Sea and North Sea (30,198 formulae; Moye et al. 2025). All formulae from raw XML peak lists were assigned using a Python formula assignment engine at 1 ppm mass accuracy against a 3.7-million-formula database. In total, 691,229 formulae across 1,343 samples were analysed.

Table 4. CRAM median log10(CIR) across seven datasets, ordered by CRAM isomeric depth. Class ordering (aromatic lowest, lipid-like highest) was conserved in all seven.

DatasetFormulaeTypeCRAM median~Isomers
WHONDRS YDE21 (Arctic)86,338Arctic sediment0.9428.7
WHONDRS S19S Sediment127,136Sediment porewater0.9518.9
WHONDRS S19S Surface Water166,262River surface (97 sites)0.9569.0
WHONDRS WROL 2019150,999Watershed resilience0.9569.0
WHONDRS 2018 Surface Water8,972River surface (27 sites)0.9889.7
Soil DOM (Xia and Liu 2025)121,324Paddy soil1.01510.4
Marine DOM (Moye et al. 2025)30,198Marine water column1.02310.5
Total691,229

The compound class ordering was conserved across all seven datasets (Table 4; Fig. 2): aromatic classes occupied the low end and lipid-like formulae the high end in every case. CRAM isomeric depth followed a gradient from Arctic sediment (0.942; ~9 isomers) through temperate river surface water and sediment porewater (0.951–0.988) to soil (1.015; ~10) and marine DOM (1.023; ~11). This gradient is broadly consistent with the expectation that more extensively processed DOM exhibits greater structural diversification per formula. The Arctic dataset (Yukon Delta) had the lowest CRAM CIR, consistent with cold-temperature-limited degradation pathways. Soil DOM (Xia and Liu 2025) showed the highest lipid-like CIR (1.327) of any dataset, likely reflecting the diversity of plant-derived lipid inputs in agricultural soils. Marine DOM was overwhelmingly CRAM (48%), consistent with the known dominance of refractory CRAM in ocean dissolved organic carbon (Hertkorn et al. 2006).

Fig. 2. Cross-ecosystem CIR comparison across seven datasets (691,229 formulae). (A) CRAM median CIR by dataset, ordered by processing intensity. Blue: river/watershed surface water; orange: sediment porewater; green: soil; purple: Arctic sediment; red: marine. (B) Class ordering (median CIR per compound class) reproduced across all seven datasets. (C) CIR density distributions.

Discussion

Chemical coherence of the class ordering

CIR orders compound classes in a manner broadly consistent with structural organic chemistry: flexible aliphatic chains (lipid-like) score highest, rigid fused ring systems (aromatics) score lowest, and alicyclic CRAM is intermediate. However, we note that under a null-penalty model (uniform constraints across all classes), the rank ordering shifts—carbohydrate-like formulae rise in rank when their additional ring and hydroxyl penalties are removed. The class ordering is therefore shaped both by underlying chemistry (carbon count, DBE, heteroatom distributions) and by the class-specific penalty structure. The key empirical results—the CRAM latitudinal gradient and the CIR–richness dissociation—are driven by within-class variation and are not sensitive to between-class ranking.

The CRAM isomeric gradient

The site-level CIRCRAM–latitude correlation (r = −0.79) is the strongest among all metrics we tested, though the margin over mean H/C is modest (|Δr| = 0.06). A plausible mechanism is that higher temperatures expand the range of kinetically accessible degradation pathways (LaRowe and Van Cappellen 2011), generating more structurally diverse CRAM products. Warmer sites also support more diverse microbial communities (Stegen et al. 2018), whose varied enzymatic repertoires would produce additional isomeric variation. Neither mechanism can be confirmed from correlational data; experimental manipulation of incubation temperature is needed.

Breadth versus depth

The negative site-level CIR–richness correlation (r = −0.40, p = 0.039), confirmed after replicate filtering at the site level (r = −0.42, p = 0.030), indicates that structural depth within formulae and compositional breadth across formulae are partially decoupled dimensions of DOM molecular complexity. This parallels the distinction between species richness and functional diversity in ecology (Petchey and Gaston 2006). Current formula-level metrics capture only the breadth dimension (Zark and Dittmar 2018; Danczak et al. 2020); CIR adds a complementary depth dimension.

Cross-ecosystem coherence

The conservation of class ordering across all seven datasets (Table 4; Fig. 2) provides the strongest evidence that CIR reflects underlying structural chemistry rather than dataset-specific artefacts. No parameters were adjusted, yet the aromatic–to–lipid gradient was reproduced in river surface water, sediment porewater, soil, Arctic sediment, and marine DOM. The CRAM isomeric depth gradient (Arctic < sediment < river < soil < marine) is broadly consistent with a processing-intensity hypothesis. Arctic DOM, subject to cold temperatures that limit the range of kinetically accessible degradation pathways, shows the lowest CRAM CIR. Marine DOM, processed for years to millennia (Dittmar and Stubbins 2014), shows the highest. Soil DOM occupies a surprisingly high position, possibly reflecting the diversity of plant-derived biosynthetic precursors and intense microbial reworking in the rhizosphere.

Practical value and limitations

CIR requires no new measurements and runs in ~2 s from standard FT-ICR-MS output. We demonstrated this portability by applying the same pipeline, with no parameter adjustments, to seven independent datasets totalling 691,229 formulae across 1,343 samples from river, sediment, soil, Arctic, and marine environments. Existing data from global surveys (Stegen et al. 2022), ocean transects (Dittmar and Stubbins 2014), and soil porewater studies (Tfaily et al. 2015) can be reanalysed to extract structural depth information that was always latent in the data but previously inaccessible.

CIR is a heuristic index. The constraint rules encode current knowledge and may underweight structural features for S- and P-containing compounds. The anchoring to TIMS (Leyva et al. 2019) is a calibration choice, not a validation; paired TIMS–FT-ICR-MS measurements of the same samples are needed to assess accuracy. The scoring coefficients (0.8, 0.7, 0.5, 0.12, 0.05) were set a priori based on chemical reasoning, not fitted to data; formal sensitivity analysis or fitting to future TIMS datasets could improve them. Finally, the one site with 63°C water (S000022, likely geothermally influenced) was retained; excluding it did not qualitatively change any result.

Conclusions

CIR is a heuristic, formula-based estimate of chemically plausible isomeric depth that captures variation not contained in formula richness alone and reveals a strong site-level latitudinal pattern within the CRAM fraction. Applied to the WHONDRS network with properly non-pseudoreplicated statistics (n = 27 sites), it shows that the isomeric richness of CRAM tracks latitude at r = −0.79—the strongest single-variable correlation among all metrics tested—and that structural depth and compositional breadth are partially decoupled axes of DOM molecular complexity. Cross-ecosystem application to seven datasets spanning 691,229 formulae from river, sediment, soil, Arctic, and marine environments confirmed the class ordering without parameter adjustment and revealed a CRAM isomeric depth gradient (Arctic < sediment < river < soil < marine) consistent with processing intensity. CIR is computationally inexpensive, reproducible, and applicable to the large archive of existing FT-ICR-MS datasets. Validation against paired TIMS measurements and experimental tests of the CIR–bioavailability relationship are the natural next steps.

Acknowledgments

WHONDRS data were obtained from ESS-DIVE. FT-ICR-MS analyses were performed at EMSL, a DOE Office of Science user facility at PNNL.

Data availability

WHONDRS data are publicly available from ESS-DIVE. The CIR pipeline (Python, 306 lines) and robustness analysis scripts are provided as Supplementary Material.
